author: Manoj Kewalramani
title: The 2026 Spike: World Order Concerns, Not Bilateral Friction
body:
Through the first quarter of 2026, Chinese rhetorical hostility towards the US sits at 63%, hovering near 2021 levels. A more granular analysis of this period may be necessary. But looking at the statements and reports covered, this appears to be more a response to broader US policy — the abduction of Nicolás Maduro and the war against Iran — reflecting concerns about the erosion of the international order rather than a product of bilateral frictions.

One should expect that with a Trump-Xi meeting taking place in May, Chinese rhetoric will ease toward a more cooperative tone while continuing to underscore red lines, both in the lead-up to and in the immediate aftermath of the summit.

author: Manoj Kewalramani
title: Why Beijing Was Softer Under Trump 2.0: Encirclement vs. Transactionalism
body:
Beijing clearly viewed the Biden administration's strategy as all-round containment. The most striking evidence of this was Xi Jinping's rare public accusation in March 2023 that the US was pursuing a campaign of "encirclement and suppression". This is not how China views the Trump administration's approach. Beijing does not believe that Trump has abandoned the framing of strategic competition, but there is clearly a perception that there is a qualitative difference between what Washington is doing under Trump and what the policy was under Biden.

Although the relationship went through an intensely rocky phase — as evidenced by the spike in hostility during the tariff shock — China's response to Trump 2.0 has been measured. Beijing's retaliatory actions in the economic domain were calibrated and reciprocal. At the same time, the Chinese leadership continued to pursue engagement: Liberation Day tariffs were imposed in early April 2025, and thereafter Chinese and American negotiators met in May, June, July, and October — in Geneva, London, Stockholm, and Kuala Lumpur — before the Xi-Trump meeting in Busan.

It is important to distinguish between rhetoric and action. While Beijing was speaking more softly compared to the past, it was also wielding a big stick, responding to American economic coercion in kind.

author: Manoj Kewalramani
title: Beijing's Grand Strategy: Managed Competition, Reactive Posture, Taiwan as the Redline
body:
The data underscores that Beijing is not approaching its relationship with the United States through the prism of a zero-sum mentality or a sense of existential crisis. Nor is it seeking a G2-style broad accommodation. Instead, what the Chinese leadership is pursuing is a strategy of managed competition, within which it seeks to advance certain key interests.

The data also shows that Beijing is far more reactive in the dyad with the US than it is proactive in shaping a new relationship. The pendulum-like shifts in rhetoric attest to this.

The foremost of Beijing's key interests is Taiwan. Party officials, right from the top, have repeatedly described Taiwan as the core of China's core interests. The push to place this front and centre likely correlates with Beijing's assessment of the relative shift in the balance of power between China and the US, the direction of America's domestic politics, and the evolving engagement between Taipei and Washington.

author: Pranay Kotasthane
title: Taiwan Is the Redline — The Data Confirms It
body:
If you want to find where China's diplomatic restraint ends, look at Taiwan. Across 641 Taiwan-related statements in this dataset, 92% are hostile — by far the highest of any topic. One in three is outright confrontational, meaning direct accusations, threats, or hostile framing. Compare this to diplomacy (39% hostile) or even trade (53% hostile). On trade, China negotiates; on technology, China protests; on Taiwan, China warns.

This isn't surprising to anyone who follows Chinese foreign policy. But the magnitude is striking. Taiwan isn't just a sensitive topic — it is categorically different from every other issue in how Chinese officials communicate about it. The implication: any data-driven read of China-US relations that doesn't distinguish Taiwan from general bilateral sentiment will systematically overstate how cooperative or tense the overall relationship is.

author: Pranay Kotasthane
title: The Attack Dogs and the Diplomats: China's Good Cop / Bad Cop Structure
body:
The data reveals a clear, structured division of labour in how China communicates toward the US. Call it institutional good cop / bad cop.

The attack dog role belongs to People's Daily editorials — particularly those published under the pseudonym 'Zhong Sheng' (钟声, meaning 'Bell Sound'). Zhong Sheng runs at 86% hostile and 41% confrontational — the most aggressive voice in this entire dataset. Regular People's Daily editorials are close behind at 82% hostile. These are the instruments China uses to signal fury without committing its named officials to positions that are hard to walk back.

By contrast, Xi Jinping himself runs at 44% hostile and 50% cooperative. His statements are more friendly than adversarial. Wang Yi, despite his reputation as a tough diplomat, sits at 49% hostile — essentially balanced. Li Qiang and Han Zheng are even softer, at 33% and 15% hostile respectively. These are the voices China leads with in bilateral summits, phone calls, and economic forums.

The architecture is deliberate. Editorial pseudonyms absorb the confrontational signalling. Leaders retain flexibility. When Beijing wants to de-escalate, it simply turns down the volume on Zhong Sheng — and it did exactly this under Trump 2.0, where Zhong Sheng's hostility dropped from 92% (under Biden) to 50%.

author: Pranay Kotasthane
title: The Tariff Shock of April 2025 — Visible in the Rhetoric
body:
Few external events leave as clean a signature in this data as Trump's April 2025 tariff escalation. In the three months before (January–March 2025), China's hostility rate held steady at 47–48% — cautious but not alarmed. Then April arrived: hostility jumped to 77%, the single highest monthly reading since mid-2021.

What followed was a controlled de-escalation. By June–July 2025, hostility had dropped back to 39–51%. This isn't a sign of Chinese capitulation — it reflects a deliberate signalling strategy. Beijing fires a loud volley to establish its position, then pulls back to create space for negotiation.

But the story doesn't end there. From December 2025 into 2026, hostility climbed again — sitting at 60–65% through early 2026. The tariff dispute may have been partially managed, but the underlying tension has not resolved. The rhetoric has settled at a new, elevated baseline.

author: Pranay Kotasthane
title: Counterintuitive Finding: China Was Softer Toward Trump 2.0 Than Biden
body:
The common assumption is that Trump's tariff aggression would produce a more hostile Chinese response than Biden's era of managed competition. The data says otherwise — at least in aggregate.

Across the Biden years (January 2021 – January 2025), 56.7% of Chinese official statements about the US were hostile, with 10% outright confrontational. Under Trump 2.0 (January 2025 to April 2026), those figures drop to 51.8% hostile and 8.3% confrontational.

Why? Several factors likely interact. First, China's hostility peaked early in the Biden era — 2021 saw 65% hostile statements, reflecting the continuation of Trump 1.0 tensions into a new administration that Beijing was still testing. Second, Trump's transactionalism is something Beijing understands and can work with. The Xi-Trump phone call in early 2026 generated some of the most cooperative language in the entire dataset. Third, when Trump escalated with tariffs in April 2025, Beijing's rhetorical spike was sharp but short — a controlled burst, not sustained hostility.

The caveat: early 2026 shows re-escalating rhetoric (60–65% hostile). Trump 2.0 is not yet over, and the aggregate may shift as the trade conflict deepens.

author: Pranay Kotasthane
title: Five Years of China-US Rhetoric: The Trend Is Not What You'd Expect
body:
The conventional wisdom is that China has become increasingly assertive over the past five years. The rhetoric data tells a more complicated story.

Hostility in Chinese statements about the US peaked in 2021 at 65%, then steadily declined: 61% in 2022, 52% in 2023, 50% in 2024, 50% in 2025. Average tone intensity (on a 1–5 scale) followed the same trajectory, falling from 3.21 in 2021 to 2.97 in 2025. By this measure, China's rhetoric toward the US was at its most measured in 2025 — not its most aggressive.

The early Biden years (mid-2021 especially) were the high-water mark for hostility, driven partly by residual tension from the Trump 1.0 trade war and partly by flashpoints over Xinjiang, Hong Kong, and the Anchorage summit. The gradual moderation through 2023–2024 coincided with a period of managed re-engagement — the Bali summit, Yellen's Beijing visit, the Biden-Xi San Francisco meeting.

But 2026 has snapped back. Through the first quarter of 2026, hostility sits at 63% — back near 2021 levels. Whether this is a new plateau or a temporary spike driven by tariff escalation remains to be seen. For now, the long arc of Chinese rhetoric toward the US looks less like linear assertiveness and more like a pendulum — with the current moment at the hostile end of the swing.
